message.content:
You are an adversarial reliability auditor. Audit /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay for RELIABILITY, ERROR HANDLING, and ROBUSTNESS issues.

Focus areas:
- Bare except clauses or exception swallowing that hides failures
- Missing error propagation: do exceptions from seams surface to the caller?
- Retry logic: are transient failures retried? Are permanent failures retried forever?
- Timeout enforcement: DB migrations, network calls, LLM calls - all need bounds
- Type validation gaps: can None/wrong-type inputs reach deep into the pipeline?
- Corpus/FeatureMatrix edge cases: empty corpus, single-element, NaN/Inf values
- Null distribution edge cases: empty list, single value, all-identical values
- Baseline hash collision or mismatch handling
- Recovery from partial pipeline failure (which phases are idempotent?)
- Pipeline ADJUDICATE/SCORE/REPORT error paths

Read src/assay_engine/pipeline.py, src/assay_engine/contracts/, src/assay_engine/methodology/ carefully.

Return findings as JSON with id (R-N), severity, file, line, title, description, poc, suggested_fix.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	"""External-claims source interface — optional, and quarantined from the baseline.
2	
3	If a dataset ships claims asserted by some external authority (relationships, labels, a
4	taxonomy, mappings), an adapter exposes them through :class:`ExternalClaimsSource`. The
5	engine converts each :class:`ClaimRecord` into a typed, falsifiable hypothesis and tests it
6	against the baseline — which is kept blind to these claims (Firewall A).
7	
8	This interface is *structurally separable*: the baseline pipeline never receives it, so the
9	baseline can be produced with claims withheld. Pure-discovery datasets omit it entirely.
10	Access to a claims source while claim-blindness is in force raises ``FirewallViolation``
11	(see :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.firewalls`).
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
17	from typing import Any, Iterable, Mapping, Protocol
18	
19	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
20	
21	
22	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
23	class ClaimRecord:
24	    """One external, expert-asserted claim about the data — a falsifiable assertion.
25	
26	    The engine treats this as a *hypothesis source*, never as ground truth. ``subject`` and
27	    ``referents`` name the units the claim is about; ``assertion`` describes what is claimed
28	    in a typed, machine-readable way; ``provenance`` records who asserted it and where from.
29	    """
30	
31	    claim_id: str
32	    subject: str
33	    referents: tuple[str, ...]
34	    assertion: Mapping[str, Any]
35	    provenance: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
36	
37	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
38	        object.__setattr__(self, "assertion", freeze_mapping(self.assertion))
39	        object.__setattr__(self, "provenance", freeze_mapping(self.provenance))
40	
41	
42	class ExternalClaimsSource(Protocol):
43	    """Adapter-provided access to a dataset's external claims (adjudication mode only)."""
44	
45	    def claims(self) -> Iterable[ClaimRecord]:
46	        """Yield every external claim to be adjudicated against the blind baseline."""
47	        ...
48	
49	    def claim_fingerprint(self) -> str:
50	        """Stable content hash of the claim set, for provenance and pre-registration."""
51	        ...
52

1	"""Study runner — the composed, governed, end-to-end pipeline (ADR-0010).
2	
3	The phase machine (:mod:`assay_engine.orchestration.phases`) *names* the ordered stages a
4	study moves through; the firewall runners enforce the methodology; the seams provide tracing,
5	experiment tracking, data versioning, and (in the adapter's callables) reasoning. This module
6	composes them into a single runnable flow, engine-owned so the ordering and the governance
7	handoffs are guaranteed once, not re-implemented per study:
8	
9	    INGEST → BASELINE(blind, Firewall A) → [DISCOVERY → PREREGISTER → gate → CONFIRM (Firewall B)]
10	           → [ADJUDICATE → SCORE (Firewall A, shared baseline)] → REPORT
11	
12	At every step it (a) records an append-only provenance entry *before the next step runs*
13	(GOVERNANCE §3), (b) opens a trace span (observability seam), optionally (c) versions the source
14	and logs to an experiment tracker, and (d) enforces the methodological invariants structurally:
15	the baseline is built blind, the split ids are validated against the corpus, hypotheses are
16	verifiably pre-registered before confirmation, a human gate reviews before *every* confirmatory
17	step, claim-derived hypotheses are locked before the baseline exists, and the visited phase
18	sequence must equal the mode's required sequence.
19	
20	A study supplies the *domain* via a :class:`StudyPlan` (parser, baseline builder, the
21	discover/confirm/hypothesis_for callables, the claims source, optional feature builders); the
22	engine owns the *order, the firewalls, the gate, the provenance, the tracing, and the
23	versioning*. Reasoning enters only inside the study's own callables (which may use
24	:mod:`assay_engine.reasoning`); any such LLM output is *judgment*, so it should be wrapped as an
25	:class:`~assay_engine.methodology.fence.Interpretation` and never fed back into a measurement
26	(the fence). The runner imports no adapter (ARCHITECTURE §3).
27	"""
28

30

81	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
82	class GateReview:
83	    """The packet handed to a gate handler at a governance transition.
84	
85	    ``payload`` is a deep-frozen :class:`~assay_engine._frozen.FrozenDict` (immutable +
86	    hashable, #113). Because that is a ``Mapping`` and not a ``dict`` subclass, ``json.dumps``
87	    cannot serialize it directly — an operator-review handler that logs/serializes the payload
88	    must call :meth:`payload_dict` first (#141).
89	    """
90	
91	    gate: str
92	    frm: Phase
93	    to: Phase
94	    summary: str
95	    payload: Mapping[str, Any]
96	
97	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
98	        # deep-freeze the payload so this frozen record is truly immutable + hashable (#113)
99	        object.__setattr__(self, "payload", freeze_mapping(self.payload))
100	
101	    def payload_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
102	        """Return the payload as a plain, deeply-thawed, JSON-serializable ``dict`` (#141)."""
103	        return cast("dict[str, Any]", unfreeze(self.payload))
104	
105	
106	GateHandler = Callable[[GateReview], GateDecision]
107	
108	
109	def _engine_claim_fingerprint(claim_records: list[ClaimRecord]) -> str:
110	    """Engine-computed content hash of the EXACT claim set that will be scored (#137).
111	
112	    The source's self-reported ``claim_fingerprint()`` is an unverified self-report — it can say
113	    anything. The engine therefore computes its own type-faithful hash over the materialized
114	    records (in scored order) and records THAT as the authoritative fingerprint, so an auditor
115	    re-deriving the fingerprint from the recorded/scored claims gets the value the trail records.
116	    """
117	    return hash_value(
118	        [
119	            {
120	                "claim_id": c.claim_id,
121	                "subject": c.subject,
122	                "referents": list(c.referents),
123	                "assertion": c.assertion,
124	                "provenance": c.provenance,
125	            }
126	            for c in claim_records
127	        ]
128	    )
129	
130	
131	def _require_unique_ids(ids: list[str], what: str) -> None:
132	    """Raise FirewallViolation if ``ids`` contains a duplicate (#138).
133	
134	    Identity (claim↔hypothesis↔verdict) must be unique within a run: a repeated id would
135	    otherwise be counted once per occurrence, inflating the scorecard denominator and biasing
136	    alignment_rate / verdict accounting.
137	    """
138	    seen: set[str] = set()
139	    dupes: list[str] = []
140	    for i in ids:
141	        if i in seen:
142	            dupes.append(i)
143	        seen.add(i)
144	    if dupes:
145	        raise FirewallViolation(
146	            f"duplicate {what} within one run: {sorted(set(dupes))[:3]} — identity must be "
147	            "unique (a repeat would inflate the scorecard denominator / verdict accounting)"
148	        )
149	
150	
151	def auto_approve(review: GateReview) -> GateDecision:
152	    """A non-interactive gate handler: approve and record the decision.
153	
154	    This is the explicit "no operator" choice — it is **not** the default, so a study cannot
155	    silently bypass human review by omitting a handler (it must consciously pass this). Replace
156	    with an operator-driven handler (e.g. one wired to the LangGraph gate interrupt/resume in
157	    :mod:`assay_engine.orchestration`) for genuine human-in-the-loop parking; the handler may
158	    return ``approved=False`` to block the transition.
159	    """
160	    return GateDecision(
161	        approved=True,
162	        gate=review.gate,
163	        reason="non-interactive auto-approval",
164	        evidence={"transition": f"{review.frm.name}->{review.to.name}"},
165	    )
166	
167	
168	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
169	class StudyPlan:
170	    """Everything the runner needs for one run: the static definition + executable methodology.
171	
172	    ``definition`` is the adapter's binding (parser, modes, claims source, questions, optional
173	    feature builders). The callables below are the adapter's *executable* methodology, validated
174	    against the declared modes: discovery needs ``split``/``discover``/``confirm_held_out``;
175	    adjudication needs ``hypothesis_for``/``confirm_claim`` (and a ``claims_source`` on the
176	    definition).
177	    """
178	
179	    definition: StudyDefinition
180	    source: Path
181	    baseline_builder: BaselineBuilder
182	    authority: TimestampAuthority
183	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit | None = None
184	    discover: DiscoverFn | None = None
185	    confirm_held_out: HeldOutConfirmer | None = None
186	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis] | None = None
187	    confirm_claim: ClaimConfirmer | None = None
188	
189	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
190	        modes = self.definition.modes
191	        if StudyMode.DISCOVERY in modes:
192	            missing = [
193	                n
194	                for n, v in (
195	                    ("split", self.split),
196	                    ("discover", self.discover),
197	                    ("confirm_held_out", self.confirm_held_out),
198	                )
199	                if v is None
200	            ]
201	            if missing:
202	                raise ValueError(f"DISCOVERY mode requires: {', '.join(missing)}")
203	        if StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS in modes:
204	            missing = [
205	                n
206	                for n, v in (
207	                    ("hypothesis_for", self.hypothesis_for),
208	                    ("confirm_claim", self.confirm_claim),
209	                )
210	                if v is None
211	            ]
212	            if missing:
213	                raise ValueError(f"ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS mode requires: {', '.join(missing)}")
214	
215	
216	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
217	class StudyResult:
218	    """The reproducibility package: the artifacts + the append-only trail that produced them."""
219	
220	    study: str
221	    corpus_fingerprint: str
222	    baseline: BaselineArtifact
223	    discovery_verdicts: tuple[Verdict, ...]
224	    scorecard: SourceScorecard | None
225	    phases: tuple[Phase, ...]
226	    provenance: tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]
227	    source_version: str | None = None
228	    feature_matrices: tuple[FeatureMatrix, ...] = ()
229	    experiment_run_id: str | None = None
230	
231	
232	def _count_logger(key: str, n: float) -> Callable[[ExperimentTracker, str], None]:
233	    """A typed metric-logging callback for :func:`run_study`'s best-effort tracker."""
234	
235	    def _log(t: ExperimentTracker, rid: str) -> None:
236	        t.log_metric(rid, key, n)
237	
238	    return _log
239	
240	
241	def _now() -> _dt.datetime:
242	    # Ordering instants are a security/methodology check and ALWAYS use real wall-clock — never
243	    # a test-injected clock (that is only for deterministic provenance hashes).
244	    return _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC)
245	
246	
247	def run_study(
248	    plan: StudyPlan,
249	    *,
250	    gate_handler: GateHandler,
251	    tracer: Tracer | None = None,
252	    tracker: ExperimentTracker | None = None,
253	    versioner: DataVersioner | None = None,
254	    clock: Clock | None = None,
255	    secret: bytes | None = None,
256	    trail: ProvenanceTrail | None = None,
257	) -> StudyResult:
258	    """Run one study end-to-end through its governed phase sequence. See module docstring.
259	
260	    ``gate_handler`` is **required** (no default), so governance review is a conscious choice —
261	    pass :func:`auto_approve` to opt out of human review explicitly, or an operator handler that
262	    can block/park. ``tracer`` defaults to :class:`OtelTracer` (real spans if OpenTelemetry + a
263	    provider are present, a graceful no-op otherwise). ``tracker`` (optional) logs params/metrics
264	    to an experiment store, best-effort (a tracker failure never aborts the run). ``versioner``
265	    (optional) content-addresses the source so the trail cites retrievable bytes. ``clock``
266	    injects a deterministic clock for the provenance timestamps only (ordering checks always use
267	    real time). ``secret`` keys the internal provenance trail with HMAC (forgery-resistant);
268	    mutually exclusive with passing your own ``trail``. ``trail`` may be a caller-owned
269	    :class:`ProvenanceTrail` so a run that *raises* still leaves the caller the partial trail.
270	
271	    Raises ``IngestionError`` on a bad source, ``FirewallViolation``/``PreRegistrationError`` on
272	    a methodological breach, ``GateError`` if a gate blocks, and ``ValueError`` on a malformed run.
273	    """
274	    tracer = tracer or OtelTracer()
275	    if trail is not None and secret is not None:
276	        raise ValueError("pass either a caller-owned trail OR a secret to key a new one, not both")
277	    defn = plan.definition
278	    modes = defn.modes
279	    required = required_phases(modes)
280	    trail = trail if trail is not None else ProvenanceTrail(secret=secret, clock=clock)
281	    visited: list[Phase] = []
282	    run_id: str | None = None
283	
284	    def enter(phase: Phase, **payload: Any) -> None:
285	        visited.append(phase)
286	        trail.record("phase", f"enter {phase.name}", phase=phase.name, **payload)
287	
288	    def track(fn: Callable[[ExperimentTracker, str], None]) -> None:
289	        # observability is best-effort: a tracker failure is recorded but never aborts a run.
290	        if tracker is None or run_id is None:
291	            return
292	        try:
293	            fn(tracker, run_id)
294	        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — tracker is an external, optional backend
295	            trail.record("tracking_error", f"experiment tracker call failed: {exc}")
296	
297	    def run_gate(review: GateReview) -> None:
298	        # Record the decision once (snapshot approved/reason) and block on rejection (#94).
299	        decision = gate_handler(review)
300	        approved = bool(getattr(decision, "approved", False))
301	        reason = str(getattr(decision, "reason", ""))
302	        trail.record(
303	            "gate",
304	            f"gate {review.gate!r}: {'approved' if approved else 'blocked'}",
305	            gate=review.gate,
306	            approved=approved,
307	            reason=reason,
308	            transition=f"{review.frm.name}->{review.to.name}",
309	        )
310	        if not approved:
311	            raise GateError(
312	                f"gate {review.gate!r} blocked {review.frm.name}->{review.to.name}: {reason}"
313	            )
314	
315	    trail.record(
316	        "run_start",
317	        f"study {defn.name!r} starting",
318	        study=defn.name,

354	            # that returns None/list/dict would otherwise slip past the normalizer above and die

356	            # "Raises IngestionError on a bad source" contract (#134).

358	                raise IngestionError(f"parser returned {type(corpus).__name__}, expected Corpus")

361	                    f"parser.source_fingerprint returned {type(source_fp).__name__}, expected str"
362	                )
363	            if not corpus.units:
364	                raise IngestionError("ingestion produced an empty corpus")

367	            if versioner is not None:

384	                    raise FirewallViolation(

1	"""Feature-builder interface — optional dataset-specific features.
2	
3	The baseline toolkit provides generic builders (embeddings, similarity, etc.). When a
4	dataset has features only meaningful in its own domain, an adapter supplies them through a
5	:class:`FeatureBuilder`. Output is a plain numeric :class:`FeatureMatrix` keyed by unit id,
6	so the engine consumes it without knowing what the features mean.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	from dataclasses import dataclass
12	from typing import Protocol, Sequence
13	
14	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
15	
16	
17	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
18	class FeatureMatrix:
19	    """Numeric features for a set of units. ``rows[i]`` corresponds to ``unit_ids[i]``."""
20	
21	    unit_ids: tuple[str, ...]
22	    feature_names: tuple[str, ...]
23	    rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...]
24	
25	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
26	        if len(self.rows) != len(self.unit_ids):
27	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix row count must match unit_ids length")
28	        width = len(self.feature_names)
29	        if any(len(r) != width for r in self.rows):
30	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix rows must match feature_names width")
31	        # Enforce the numeric type contract the docstring promises: a non-numeric cell (a str,
32	        # None, NaN) from a misimplemented builder would otherwise flow into baseline math and
33	        # surface as an opaque downstream error. bool is excluded (it is an int subclass but not
34	        # a feature value). NaN/inf are rejected as not finite numeric features (#149).
35	        import math
36	
37	        for i, r in enumerate(self.rows):
38	            for v in r:
39	                if isinstance(v, bool) or not isinstance(v, (int, float)):
40	                    raise TypeError(
41	                        f"FeatureMatrix.rows[{i}] contains a non-numeric value {v!r} "
42	                        f"({type(v).__name__}); features must be int/float"
43	                    )
44	                if not math.isfinite(v):
45	                    raise ValueError(f"FeatureMatrix.rows[{i}] contains a non-finite value {v!r}")
46	
47	
48	class FeatureBuilder(Protocol):
49	    """Build dataset-specific numeric features from a canonical corpus."""
50	
51	    def build(self, corpus: Corpus) -> FeatureMatrix:
52	        """Compute features for (a subset of) the corpus units. Must be deterministic."""
53	        ...
54	
55	    @property
56	    def provides(self) -> Sequence[str]:
57	        """Names of the features this builder produces (for provenance)."""
58	        ...
59

1	"""Canonical schema — the dataset-agnostic shape the baseline is built from.
2	
3	An adapter's job is to turn its raw source into a :class:`Corpus` of these types. The engine
4	builds the baseline from a ``Corpus`` and never sees the source's native format.
5	
6	Firewall A boundary (an adapter obligation, not a type-enforced guarantee):
7	    A ``Corpus`` carries the *data itself* — units of text/attributes and any structure that
8	    is intrinsic to the raw source. It must **not** carry an external authority's *judgments*
9	    about the data (asserted relationships, strengths, labels, a taxonomy). Those are claims;
10	    they travel through :mod:`assay_engine.contracts.claims`, quarantined from baseline
11	    construction. Putting an external mapping into ``Corpus.relations`` (or external labels
12	    into ``attributes``/``metadata``) is a Firewall A violation.
13	
14	    Because the mapping/relation payloads are deliberately open (the engine is
15	    dataset-agnostic), this boundary is an **adapter contract obligation** the type system
16	    cannot enforce — not a structural guarantee. The structural guarantees of Firewall A are
17	    that the baseline builder is never handed an ``ExternalClaimsSource`` and that
18	    ``StudyDefinition`` refuses a claims source outside adjudication mode (audit pass 1,
19	    issues #1, #3).
20	"""
21	
22	from __future__ import annotations
23	
24	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
25	from typing import Any, Mapping
26	
27	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
28	
29	
30	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
31	class Unit:
32	    """One analyzable object in the corpus (a document, record, entity, item).
33	
34	    ``unit_id`` is stable and unique within the corpus. ``text`` is the natural-language
35	    content the NLP baseline operates on (may be empty for non-text units). ``attributes``
36	    holds structured fields intrinsic to the data — never an external authority's verdicts.
37	    """
38	
39	    unit_id: str
40	    text: str = ""
41	    attributes: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
42	    source_ref: str | None = None
43	
44	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
45	        object.__setattr__(self, "attributes", freeze_mapping(self.attributes))
46	
47	
48	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
49	class Relation:
50	    """A relationship that is *intrinsic to the raw data* (not an external judgment).
51	
52	    Example of legitimate use: a parent/child link the source file itself encodes. Example of
53	    illegitimate use: an external expert's asserted mapping between units — that is a claim
54	    (see :mod:`assay_engine.contracts.claims`) and must be withheld from the baseline.
55	    """
56	
57	    source_id: str
58	    target_id: str
59	    kind: str
60	    attributes: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
61	
62	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
63	        object.__setattr__(self, "attributes", freeze_mapping(self.attributes))
64	
65	
66	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
67	class Corpus:
68	    """The full canonical dataset handed to the baseline builder."""
69	
70	    units: tuple[Unit, ...]
71	    relations: tuple[Relation, ...] = ()
72	    metadata: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
73	
74	    def unit_ids(self) -> frozenset[str]:
75	        return frozenset(u.unit_id for u in self.units)
76	
77	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
78	        ids = [u.unit_id for u in self.units]
79	        if len(ids) != len(set(ids)):
80	            raise ValueError("Corpus contains duplicate unit_id values")
81	        # A closed canonical corpus must not contain relations to non-existent units
82	        # (audit pass 1, issue #3) — mirrors the duplicate-id and FeatureMatrix checks.
83	        known = set(ids)
84	        dangling = sorted(
85	            {e for r in self.relations for e in (r.source_id, r.target_id) if e not in known}
86	        )
87	        if dangling:
88	            raise ValueError(f"Corpus has relations referencing unknown unit_id(s): {dangling[:5]}")
89	        object.__setattr__(self, "metadata", freeze_mapping(self.metadata))
90

1	"""Adjudication runner — the engine owns the firewall guarantee, not each study (ADR-0008).
2	
3	METHODOLOGY.md describes the adjudication pipeline: build a baseline *blind* to the external
4	claims (Firewall A), convert each claim into a typed, pre-stated hypothesis, test each against
5	the blind baseline, and *score the external source* against the validated baseline (§5). Until
6	now the engine shipped the parts (a custodial :class:`ClaimBlindGuard`, the confirmatory tests,
7	the verdicts) but no composition — so Firewall A held only if a study wired it correctly by
8	hand. For a blueprint inherited by many studies, that pushes the single most important
9	methodological guarantee onto every cloner, where it is easy to get silently wrong (build the
10	baseline with the claims in scope and nothing stops you).
11	
12	:func:`adjudicate` makes the guarantee structural: it builds the baseline *inside* a sealed
13	claim-guard that holds the claims source, so the baseline builder cannot reach the claims
14	during construction (any attempt raises ``FirewallViolation``); only after the baseline is
15	built does the runner release the claims and adjudicate. It then aggregates the verdicts into a
16	:class:`SourceScorecard` — the §5 "score the source" step, expressed as alignment *frequency*
17	on decisive claims, explicitly NOT a normative judgement of the source's quality (richer
18	dimensions — where it aligns, diverges, leaves gaps — are data-surfaced downstream, per §5).
19	"""
20	
21	from __future__ import annotations
22	
23	import datetime as _dt
24	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
25	from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Callable, Iterable, Protocol
26	
27	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ClaimRecord, ExternalClaimsSource
28	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
29	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard, FirewallViolation
30	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisOrigin
31	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
32	    TimestampAuthority,
33	    require_preregistered,
34	)
35	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
36	
37	if TYPE_CHECKING:
38	    # Annotations only — importing baseline at runtime would invert the layering
39	    # (baseline.toolkit depends on methodology.firewalls) and create a cycle.
40	    from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact, BaselineBuilder
41	
42	
43	class ClaimConfirmer(Protocol):
44	    """Study-supplied: confirm one claim-derived hypothesis against the (blind) baseline.
45	
46	    The study owns this because only it knows the baseline's structure and how a given claim
47	    maps onto a measurement of it; the engine supplies the confirmatory primitives
48	    (``confirm_whole_corpus`` / ``confirm_unit_level``) the study uses inside it.
49	    """
50	
51	    def __call__(
52	        self, hypothesis: Hypothesis, baseline: BaselineArtifact, claim: ClaimRecord
53	    ) -> Verdict: ...
54	
55	
56	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
57	class SourceScorecard:
58	    """How an external source aligns with the independent baseline (METHODOLOGY.md §5).
59	
60	    ``alignment_rate`` is supported / (supported + contradicted) — the fraction of *decisive*
61	    claims where the blind baseline agrees with the source's assertion. ``indeterminate`` is
62	    excluded from the denominator (the method could not decide; counting it would understate
63	    or overstate alignment). This is a frequency, not a verdict on whether the source is
64	    "good": it says how often an independent reading of the data corroborates the source.
65	    """
66	
67	    source: str
68	    n_supported: int
69	    n_contradicted: int
70	    n_indeterminate: int
71	    alignment_rate: float | None
72	    verdicts: tuple[Verdict, ...] = field(default_factory=tuple)
73	
74	    @property
75	    def total(self) -> int:
76	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted + self.n_indeterminate
77	
78	    @property
79	    def decisive(self) -> int:
80	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted
81	
82	
83	def _score(source: str, verdicts: list[Verdict]) -> SourceScorecard:
84	    counts = {
85	        VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED: 0,
86	        VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED: 0,
87	        VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE: 0,
88	    }
89	    for v in verdicts:
90	        counts[v.label] += 1
91	    decisive = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] + counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED]
92	    rate = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] / decisive if decisive else None
93	    return SourceScorecard(
94	        source=source,
95	        n_supported=counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED],
96	        n_contradicted=counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED],
97	        n_indeterminate=counts[VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE],
98	        alignment_rate=rate,
99	        verdicts=tuple(verdicts),
100	    )
101	
102	
103	def adjudicate(
104	    corpus: Corpus,
105	    claims_source: ExternalClaimsSource,
106	    *,
107	    baseline_builder: BaselineBuilder,
108	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
109	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
110	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
111	    source_name: str = "external",
112	) -> tuple[BaselineArtifact, SourceScorecard]:
113	    """Run the full adjudication, enforcing Firewall A by construction.
114	
115	    1. The claims source is kept in this function's local scope and is NOT handed to the
116	       builder; the baseline is built inside a sealed :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` that holds
117	       nothing. This is a **signature-level** guarantee: the builder is never *handed* a
118	       claims source by the engine, so it cannot accidentally consult the claims. It is NOT
119	       frame isolation — a builder that deliberately reflects into this runner's call stack
120	       (``sys._getframe``) could reach ``claims_source``; preventing that would require
121	       running the builder in a separate process, which is out of scope. The guarantee is
122	       against *accidental* circularity, not against a builder that willfully defeats the
123	       firewall.
124	    2. After the baseline exists, each claim is mapped (by ``hypothesis_for``) to its typed,
125	       pre-stated ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` hypothesis whose ``source_claim_id`` must match the claim
126	       and which must be genuinely **pre-registered** — :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.
127	       preregistration.require_preregistered` checks (via the supplied ``authority``) that the
128	       proof binds this hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes the
129	       **baseline build** (the ordering instant is captured before the build). A claim-derived
130	       hypothesis must therefore be locked *before* ``adjudicate`` is called — ``hypothesis_for``
131	       looks up an already-locked hypothesis; locking on demand inside it (after the baseline
132	       exists) is rejected, so the hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline.
133	    3. The verdict's ``hypothesis_id`` must match the hypothesis it answers (no misattribution),
134	       and the verdicts are aggregated into a :class:`SourceScorecard`.
135	
136	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
137	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
138	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default, so a study must supply a real one.
139	
140	    Returns ``(baseline, scorecard)``. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl.
141	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.PreRegistrationError`) on any
142	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity mismatch, a non-``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` (data-surfaced)
143	    hypothesis, or a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered before confirmation.
144	    """
145	    # The claims source stays in THIS function's local scope and is never given to the builder.
146	    # The builder receives a guard holding nothing (a blind-mode signal only), so there is no
147	    # object reachable from the builder that contains the claims — not even via the guard's
148	    # internals. This is stronger than a custodial guard, whose private `_claims_source` a
149	    # determined builder could read past the sealed `release()` check (adversarial review).
150	    # Captured BEFORE the baseline is built: an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis derives from the
151	    # external claim, not from the baseline, so it must be pre-registered before the baseline (the
152	    # answer) exists. Using this as `not_after` makes "lock before the result is in hand" real —
153	    # a hypothesis locked on demand inside `hypothesis_for` (i.e. after the build) is rejected,
154	    # forcing studies to pre-lock their claim-derived hypotheses (#83).
155	    not_after = _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
156	
157	    guard: ClaimBlindGuard[ExternalClaimsSource] = ClaimBlindGuard()
158	    with guard.sealed():
159	        baseline = baseline_builder.build(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
160	
161	    scorecard = adjudicate_with_baseline(
162	        baseline,
163	        claims_source.claims(),
164	        hypothesis_for=hypothesis_for,
165	        confirm=confirm,
166	        authority=authority,
167	        not_after=not_after,
168	        source_name=source_name,
169	    )
170	    return baseline, scorecard
171	
172	
173	def adjudicate_with_baseline(
174	    baseline: BaselineArtifact,
175	    claims: "Iterable[ClaimRecord]",
176	    *,
177	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
178	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
179	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
180	    not_after: _dt.datetime,
181	    source_name: str = "external",
182	    on_step: "Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None" = None,
183	) -> SourceScorecard:
184	    """Adjudicate a **materialized claim set** against an already-built blind baseline (core).
185	
186	    Factored out of :func:`adjudicate` so a composed pipeline that builds the baseline once
187	    (and shares it with discovery) can reuse the identical, hardened Firewall-A loop instead of
188	    rebuilding the baseline. The caller is responsible for having built ``baseline`` blind and
189	    for passing the ``not_after`` ordering instant captured *before* that build — so the
190	    lock-before-baseline guarantee is preserved across the shared-baseline composition.
191	
192	    ``claims`` is **materialized once** here (not re-pulled from a source): a caller that gated
193	    or recorded a claim snapshot must adjudicate *that* snapshot, not a set a re-iterable source
194	    could change between the gate and the scoring (adversarial review #95). Enforces the same
195	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity + pre-registration checks as :func:`adjudicate`.
196	    """
197	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
198	    materialized = list(claims)  # claims used only after the blind baseline is built
199	    # within-run identity uniqueness: a repeated claim_id would be counted once per occurrence in
200	    # _score, inflating the scorecard denominator and biasing alignment_rate (#138).
201	    seen_claim_ids: set[str] = set()
202	    for c in materialized:
203	        if c.claim_id in seen_claim_ids:
204	            raise FirewallViolation(
205	                f"duplicate claim_id {c.claim_id!r} within one adjudication run — claim identity "
206	                "must be unique (a repeat would inflate the scorecard denominator)"
207	            )
208	        seen_claim_ids.add(c.claim_id)
209	    for claim in materialized:
210	        hypothesis = hypothesis_for(claim)
211	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM:
212	            raise FirewallViolation(
213	                f"claim {claim.claim_id!r} produced a {hypothesis.origin.value!r} hypothesis; "
214	                "adjudicated claims must be EXTERNAL_CLAIM (pre-stated, not data-surfaced)"
215	            )
216	        if hypothesis.source_claim_id != claim.claim_id:
217	            raise FirewallViolation(
218	                f"hypothesis for claim {claim.claim_id!r} carries source_claim_id "
219	                f"{hypothesis.source_claim_id!r} — claim↔hypothesis misattribution"
220	            )
221	        # Pre-registration: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content (no post-lock content
222	        # swap) and its attested lock time must precede the baseline build (so the claim-derived
223	        # hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline).
224	        require_preregistered(hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=not_after)
225	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, baseline, claim)
226	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
227	            raise FirewallViolation(
228	                f"verdict for hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} reports hypothesis_id "
229	                f"{verdict.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
230	            )
231	        verdicts.append(verdict)
232	        if on_step is not None:
233	            # per-claim hook (in order) so a caller can record the pre-registered hypothesis AND
234	            # its verdict to provenance as each happens, not in a post-hoc batch (#93).
235	            on_step(hypothesis, verdict)
236	
237	    return _score(source_name, verdicts)
238

1	"""The two firewalls, enforced as runtime primitives (ADR-0005, METHODOLOGY.md §2).
2	
3	Both separations are enforced in code and exercised by tests — never asserted only in prose.
4	
5	Firewall A — claim-blindness:
6	    The baseline must be built blind to external claims. The *structural* guarantee is that
7	    the baseline builder is never handed an ``ExternalClaimsSource`` (its ``build`` signature
8	    takes only a ``Corpus``) and that ``StudyDefinition`` refuses a claims source outside
9	    adjudication mode. On top of that, :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` gives the claims source
10	    *custody*: hand the source to the guard, and the only way to obtain it is
11	    ``release()``/``access()``, which raise while the guard is sealed. Baseline construction
12	    runs inside ``sealed()``; adjudication runs after it.
13	
14	    Caveat (audit pass 1, issue #1): the guard cannot police a baseline builder that ignores
15	    it and reads external judgments smuggled into ``Corpus`` metadata/relations — that path
16	    is closed by the adapter contract (see schema.py), not by this guard. The guard is
17	    custodial defense-in-depth over the type-level separation, not the sole barrier.
18	
19	Firewall B — discover/confirm separation:
20	    The data used to discover a hypothesis must not be the data used to confirm it.
21	    :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit` holds disjoint, non-empty discovery and confirmation id
22	    sets and refuses to confirm on any id the discovery phase touched (or on an empty set).
23	"""
24	
25	from __future__ import annotations
26	
27	from contextlib import contextmanager
28	from dataclasses import dataclass
29	from typing import Generic, Iterable, Iterator, TypeVar
30	
31	C = TypeVar("C")
32	
33	
34	class FirewallViolation(RuntimeError):
35	    """Raised when a methodological firewall is breached at runtime."""
36	
37	
38	class ClaimBlindGuard(Generic[C]):
39	    """Firewall A. While sealed, the held claims source is unreachable.
40	
41	    Custodial usage (preferred) — the guard owns the source, so the baseline pipeline holds
42	    the guard rather than the source and cannot read claims while sealed::
43	
44	        guard = ClaimBlindGuard(claims_source)
45	        with guard.sealed():
46	            corpus = parser.parse(source)            # baseline build — claims unreachable
47	            baseline = build_baseline(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
48	        claims = guard.release()                      # adjudication — guard released
49	
50	    ``access(obj)`` remains for defensive checks at call sites that hold a reference directly:
51	    it returns ``obj`` when unsealed and raises while sealed.
52	    """
53	
54	    def __init__(self, claims_source: C | None = None) -> None:
55	        self._sealed = False
56	        self._claims_source = claims_source
57	
58	    @property
59	    def is_sealed(self) -> bool:
60	        return self._sealed
61	
62	    @contextmanager
63	    def sealed(self) -> Iterator["ClaimBlindGuard[C]"]:
64	        prior = self._sealed
65	        self._sealed = True
66	        try:
67	            yield self
68	        finally:
69	            self._sealed = prior
70	
71	    def release(self) -> C:
72	        """Return the custodial claims source, or raise while sealed / if none was given."""
73	        if self._sealed:
74	            raise FirewallViolation(
75	                "Firewall A: external claims released during blind baseline construction"
76	            )
77	        if self._claims_source is None:
78	            raise FirewallViolation("Firewall A: no claims source is under this guard's custody")
79	        return self._claims_source
80	
81	    def access(self, claims_source: C) -> C:
82	        """Return ``claims_source`` only if the guard is not sealed; else raise."""
83	        if self._sealed:
84	            raise FirewallViolation(
85	                "Firewall A: external claims accessed during blind baseline construction"
86	            )
87	        return claims_source
88	
89	
90	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
91	class DiscoverConfirmSplit:
92	    """Firewall B. Disjoint discovery / confirmation id partitions for unit-level claims."""
93	
94	    discovery_ids: frozenset[str]
95	    confirm_ids: frozenset[str]
96	
97	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
98	        # Coerce to frozenset so the raw constructor (public API) cannot yield a "frozen" record
99	        # backed by mutable sets — otherwise the disjointness invariant below could be defeated by
100	        # post-construction in-place mutation (s.confirm_ids.add(...)) (#135).
101	        if not isinstance(self.discovery_ids, frozenset):
102	            object.__setattr__(self, "discovery_ids", frozenset(self.discovery_ids))
103	        if not isinstance(self.confirm_ids, frozenset):
104	            object.__setattr__(self, "confirm_ids", frozenset(self.confirm_ids))
105	        overlap = self.discovery_ids & self.confirm_ids
106	        if overlap:
107	            raise FirewallViolation(
108	                f"Firewall B: discovery and confirmation partitions overlap on "
109	                f"{len(overlap)} id(s), e.g. {sorted(overlap)[:3]}"
110	            )
111	        # An empty confirmation partition means there is nothing held out to confirm on; a
112	        # unit-level held-out split with no held-out object is invalid by construction. The
113	        # genuine no-holdout case must use the whole-corpus null path (audit pass 1, issue #5).
114	        if not self.confirm_ids:
115	            raise FirewallViolation(
116	                "Firewall B: confirmation partition is empty — a unit-level held-out split "
117	                "must hold out at least one object (use whole-corpus confirmation otherwise)"
118	            )
119	
120	    @classmethod
121	    def from_partition(
122	        cls, discovery_ids: Iterable[str], confirm_ids: Iterable[str]
123	    ) -> "DiscoverConfirmSplit":
124	        return cls(frozenset(discovery_ids), frozenset(confirm_ids))
125	
126	    def assert_confirm_only(self, ids: Iterable[str]) -> None:
127	        """Raise if ``ids`` is empty or any of it was seen during discovery (double-dip)."""
128	        ids = frozenset(ids)
129	        if not ids:
130	            raise FirewallViolation(
131	                "Firewall B: confirmatory test evaluated zero held-out objects — a verdict "
132	                "cannot be produced from an empty confirmation set (audit pass 1, issue #5)"
133	            )
134	        leaked = ids & self.discovery_ids
135	        if leaked:
136	            raise FirewallViolation(
137	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test touched {len(leaked)} discovery id(s) — "
138	                f"double-dipping, e.g. {sorted(leaked)[:3]}"
139	            )
140	        unknown = ids - self.confirm_ids
141	        if unknown:
142	            raise FirewallViolation(
143	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test used {len(unknown)} id(s) outside the "
144	                f"confirmation partition, e.g. {sorted(unknown)[:3]}"
145	            )
146

1	"""Dependency-light, deterministic numeric primitives — the baseline's raw material.
2	
3	These are the small, exact, NumPy-free building blocks the methodology calls "raw material"
4	for a baseline: similarity/distance and descriptive statistics over a
5	:class:`~assay_engine.contracts.features.FeatureMatrix`. They are deterministic (no RNG, no
6	iteration-order dependence) and pure, so a study can assemble a simple baseline — and the
7	engine can unit-test these invariants — with no heavy dependency.
8	
9	Studies needing performance or richer ML (embedding models, clustering, graph/topology) bring
10	their own builders implementing :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`; the
11	engine deliberately does not prescribe those choices (ADR-0002). Heavier engine helpers may be
12	added later under the optional ``baseline`` extra, but the contract here stays dependency-free.
13	"""
14	
15	from __future__ import annotations
16	
17	import math
18	from typing import Any, Sequence
19	
20	Vector = Sequence[float]
21	
22	
23	def _check_same_length(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> None:
24	    if len(a) != len(b):
25	        raise ValueError(f"vectors must have equal length; got {len(a)} and {len(b)}")
26	
27	
28	def _check_finite(*vectors: Vector) -> None:
29	    """Reject non-finite components. Without this a NaN/inf could flow into cosine and be
30	    silently clamped to a false 1.0 (audit #B6); consistent with descriptive_stats."""
31	    for vec in vectors:
32	        for x in vec:
33	            if not math.isfinite(x):
34	                raise ValueError(f"vector components must be finite; got {x}")
35	
36	
37	def dot(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
38	    _check_same_length(a, b)
39	    return math.fsum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
40	
41	
42	def l2_norm(a: Vector) -> float:
43	    return math.sqrt(math.fsum(x * x for x in a))
44	
45	
46	def _clamp_unit(x: float) -> float:
47	    """Clamp to [-1, 1] to absorb floating-point overshoot (e.g. self-similarity 1.0000…002)."""
48	    return max(-1.0, min(1.0, x))
49	
50	
51	def cosine_similarity(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
52	    """Cosine similarity in [-1, 1]. A zero vector has no direction, so its similarity to
53	    anything is defined as 0.0 (rather than NaN). The result is clamped to [-1, 1] so float
54	    rounding cannot push it outside the documented range (audit #B3)."""
55	    _check_same_length(a, b)
56	    _check_finite(a, b)
57	    na, nb = l2_norm(a), l2_norm(b)
58	    if na == 0.0 or nb == 0.0:
59	        return 0.0
60	    return _clamp_unit(dot(a, b) / (na * nb))
61	
62	
63	def euclidean_distance(a: Vector, b: Vector) -> float:
64	    _check_same_length(a, b)
65	    _check_finite(a, b)
66	    return math.sqrt(math.fsum((x - y) ** 2 for x, y in zip(a, b)))
67	
68	
69	def _cosine_matrix_pure(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
70	    """Pure-Python reference implementation — O(n²·d). Correct but slow; the public function
71	    dispatches to a numpy path at scale when available."""
72	    n = len(rows)
73	    _check_finite(*rows)
74	    norms = [l2_norm(r) for r in rows]
75	    out = [[0.0] * n for _ in range(n)]
76	    for i in range(n):
77	        for j in range(i, n):
78	            if norms[i] == 0.0 or norms[j] == 0.0:
79	                sim = 0.0
80	            elif i == j:
81	                sim = 1.0  # a non-zero vector's self-similarity is exactly 1.0 (no float drift)
82	            else:
83	                sim = _clamp_unit(dot(rows[i], rows[j]) / (norms[i] * norms[j]))
84	            out[i][j] = sim
85	            out[j][i] = sim
86	    return out
87	
88	
89	def _cosine_ndarray(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> Any:
90	    """Compute the cosine matrix as a numpy ndarray (no nested-list boxing)."""
91	    import numpy as np
92	
93	    x = np.asarray(rows, dtype=float)
94	    if x.ndim != 2:
95	        raise ValueError("rows must be a 2-D collection of equal-length vectors")
96	    if not np.isfinite(x).all():
97	        raise ValueError("vector components must be finite")
98	    norms = np.linalg.norm(x, axis=1)
99	    if not np.isfinite(norms).all():
100	        # finite inputs can still overflow when squared (||x||) on huge-magnitude vectors;
101	        # reject rather than silently produce inf/inf similarities (audit N1; restores parity
102	        # with the pure path, which raises on the same input).
103	        raise ValueError("vector norm overflow — component magnitudes are too large")
104	    zero = norms == 0.0
105	    safe = np.where(zero, 1.0, norms)
106	    xn = x / safe[:, None]
107	    m = xn @ xn.T
108	    np.clip(m, -1.0, 1.0, out=m)
109	    if zero.any():  # zero vectors have no direction → similarity 0 to everything
110	        m[zero, :] = 0.0
111	        m[:, zero] = 0.0
112	    np.fill_diagonal(m, np.where(zero, 0.0, 1.0))
113	    return m
114	
115	
116	def _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
117	    return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in _cosine_ndarray(rows)]
118	
119	
120	def cosine_similarity_matrix_array(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> Any:
121	    """The cosine matrix as a numpy ndarray — no nested-list boxing (#106).
122	
123	    For large corpora the nested-list form of :func:`cosine_similarity_matrix` multiplies peak
124	    memory several-fold; a builder operating at scale should keep the ndarray and store it in the
125	    baseline's ``contents`` (the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested
126	    list). Requires numpy (the ``baseline`` extra).
127	    """
128	    try:
129	        import numpy as np
130	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
131	        raise RuntimeError(
132	            "cosine_similarity_matrix_array requires numpy (the 'baseline' extra)"
133	        ) from exc
134	    if not rows:
135	        return np.empty((0, 0), dtype=float)
136	    return _cosine_ndarray(rows)
137	
138	
139	def cosine_similarity_matrix(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
140	    """Full symmetric cosine-similarity matrix (diagonal 1.0 for non-zero vectors, 0.0 for zero).
141	
142	    Pure-Python is O(n²·d) and does not scale (a corpus of thousands of embeddings would take
143	    minutes-to-hours); when numpy is available (the optional ``baseline`` extra) this dispatches
144	    to a vectorized path that is orders of magnitude faster. The two paths agree to within
145	    floating-point tolerance (both clamp to [-1, 1]); a study needing byte-identical
146	    reproducibility should pin its environment (the determinism harness records component
147	    versions for exactly this reason).
148	    """
149	    if not rows:
150	        return []
151	    try:
152	        import numpy  # noqa: F401
153	    except ImportError:
154	        return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)
155	    return _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows)
156	
157	
158	def _quantile(sorted_values: list[float], q: float) -> float:
159	    """Linear-interpolation quantile (``q`` in [0, 1]) over already-sorted values."""
160	    if not 0.0 <= q <= 1.0:
161	        raise ValueError(f"quantile q must be in [0, 1]; got {q}")
162	    n = len(sorted_values)
163	    if n == 1:
164	        return sorted_values[0]
165	    pos = q * (n - 1)
166	    lo = math.floor(pos)
167	    hi = math.ceil(pos)
168	    if lo == hi:
169	        return sorted_values[lo]
170	    frac = pos - lo
171	    return sorted_values[lo] * (1 - frac) + sorted_values[hi] * frac
172	
173	
174	def descriptive_stats(values: Sequence[float]) -> dict[str, float]:
175	    """Descriptive statistics of ``values``.
176	
177	    Returns ``n`` (count), ``mean``, ``std`` (sample std, ddof=1; ``0.0`` for a single value
178	    rather than NaN), ``min``, ``max``, and ``q25``/``median``/``q75`` (linear-interpolation
179	    quantiles). Raises on an empty input or any non-finite value.
180	    """
181	    if not values:
182	        raise ValueError("descriptive_stats requires at least one value")
183	    n = len(values)
184	    for v in values:
185	        if not math.isfinite(v):
186	            raise ValueError(f"values must be finite; got {v}")
187	    mean = math.fsum(values) / n
188	    if n == 1:
189	        std = 0.0
190	    else:
191	        var = math.fsum((v - mean) ** 2 for v in values) / (n - 1)
192	        std = math.sqrt(var)
193	    s = sorted(values)
194	    return {
195	        "n": float(n),
196	        "mean": mean,
197	        "std": std,
198	        "min": s[0],
199	        "max": s[-1],
200	        "q25": _quantile(s, 0.25),
201	        "median": _quantile(s, 0.5),
202	        "q75": _quantile(s, 0.75),
203	    }

1	"""Determinism & reproducibility harness for the baseline (ADR-0001, METHODOLOGY.md §1, §7).
2	
3	A baseline is only a yardstick if the *same inputs reproduce the same baseline*. This module
4	makes that contract concrete and engine-level: it content-hashes inputs, derives seeds
5	deterministically from those hashes, records component versions, and stamps all of it into a
6	:class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineArtifact`'s ``determinism`` record — so a
7	hostile reviewer can confirm a baseline was produced exactly as claimed.
8	
9	It is dependency-free and pure. The heavy, *choice-bearing* builders (which embedding model,
10	which clustering algorithm, which graph construction) encode dataset/study decisions and are
11	supplied by a study's adapter as :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`
12	implementations — not prescribed by the engine (ADR-0002). This harness records *how* such a
13	builder ran so the result is reproducible regardless of which builder it was.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import hashlib
19	import json
20	import sys
21	from dataclasses import dataclass
22	from typing import Any, Mapping
23	
24	from assay_engine import __version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION
25	
26	# The canonical hashing primitives moved to ``assay_engine._canonical`` (see note below); these
27	# imports re-export them at their historical home so existing call sites keep working.
28	from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_plain as _plain  # noqa: F401
29	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_bytes as hash_bytes  # noqa: F401  (re-export)
30	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_text
31	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_value as hash_value  # re-export (baseline public API)
32	from assay_engine._canonical import keytag as _keytag  # noqa: F401
33	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
34	from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact
35	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
36	
37	# The canonical hashing primitives (``_plain``/``_keytag``/``hash_bytes``/``hash_text``/
38	# ``hash_value``) live in ``assay_engine._canonical`` so they sit below both ``baseline`` and
39	# ``methodology`` in the import graph (audit #B1/#B2/#B5 originally landed here; hoisted so
40	# pre-registration can content-bind a hypothesis with the *same* type-faithful digest). They are
41	# re-exported above for back-compat.
42	
43	
44	def corpus_fingerprint(corpus: Corpus) -> str:
45	    """Deterministic content hash of a corpus (order-independent across units/relations)."""
46	    units = [
47	        {"id": u.unit_id, "text": u.text, "attrs": _plain(u.attributes)}
48	        for u in sorted(corpus.units, key=lambda u: u.unit_id)
49	    ]
50	    relations = [
51	        {"s": r.source_id, "t": r.target_id, "k": r.kind, "attrs": _plain(r.attributes)}
52	        for r in sorted(corpus.relations, key=lambda r: (r.source_id, r.target_id, r.kind))
53	    ]
54	    payload = {"units": units, "relations": relations, "metadata": _plain(corpus.metadata)}
55	    return hash_text(json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False))
56	
57	
58	def stable_seed(*parts: str, bits: int = 32) -> int:
59	    """A deterministic non-negative integer seed derived from ``parts``.
60	
61	    Derived from a hash so the same inputs always yield the same seed — reproducible without a
62	    hard-coded magic number, and distinct inputs get distinct seeds.
63	    """
64	    digest = hashlib.sha256("\x1f".join(parts).encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()
65	    return int(digest, 16) % (1 << bits)
66	
67	
68	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
69	class DeterminismRecord:
70	    """The reproducibility provenance stamped onto a baseline (ADR-0001)."""
71	
72	    seed: int
73	    input_hashes: Mapping[str, str]
74	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str]
75	
76	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
77	        object.__setattr__(self, "input_hashes", freeze_mapping(self.input_hashes))
78	        object.__setattr__(self, "component_versions", freeze_mapping(self.component_versions))
79	
80	    def as_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
81	        return {
82	            "seed": self.seed,
83	            "input_hashes": dict(self.input_hashes),
84	            "component_versions": dict(self.component_versions),
85	        }
86	
87	
88	def build_baseline_artifact(
89	    corpus: Corpus,
90	    contents: Mapping[str, Any],
91	    *,
92	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
93	    extra_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None,
94	    seed: int | None = None,
95	    corpus_hash: str | None = None,
96	    trust_corpus_hash: bool = False,
97	) -> BaselineArtifact:
98	    """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
99	
100	    ``contents`` is the builder's output (embeddings, similarity graph, clusters, …).
101	    ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
102	    Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
103	    (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
104	    so it is reproducible.
105	
106	    ``corpus_hash`` lets a caller pass an already-computed :func:`corpus_fingerprint` (#119). By
107	    default it is **verified** against the corpus and a mismatch raises ``ValueError`` — a
108	    stale/typo'd hash would otherwise produce a determinism record whose fingerprint and derived
109	    seed describe a *different* corpus than the one passed, silently defeating the reproducibility
110	    guarantee this harness exists to provide (#155). Pass ``trust_corpus_hash=True`` to skip the
111	    recompute (the #119 optimization) only when the caller computed the hash from this exact
112	    corpus and accepts integrity responsibility.
113	    """
114	    if corpus_hash is None:
115	        corpus_hash = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
116	    elif not trust_corpus_hash:
117	        # Verify the supplied hash binds the corpus actually passed (default-safe, #155).
118	        actual = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
119	        if corpus_hash != actual:
120	            raise ValueError(
121	                f"corpus_hash {corpus_hash!r} does not match the corpus "
122	                f"(corpus_fingerprint={actual!r}) — the determinism record would bind the wrong "
123	                "data; pass trust_corpus_hash=True only if you accept integrity responsibility"
124	            )
125	    if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
126	        raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key — rename the extra input (#B7)")
127	    input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}
128	    for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items():
129	        input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
130	
131	    versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, "python": sys.version.split()[0]}
132	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
133	
134	    if seed is None:
135	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
136	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
137	
138	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
139	    return BaselineArtifact(
140	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
141	    )
